text:
Here's the long and short of it, Iran has been a global terrorist state for 47 years, during that time the opportunity to do something about it has been very limited to avoid another Iraq or Afghanistan. On a global level, Iran is the primary supplier of drones to Russia, oil to China, and terror training and equipment to Muslim extremists across the world particularly in the Middle East in their offense by proxy strategy against Israel and the moderate Islamic nations. The stars have finally aligned with the US opening Venezuela to the oil market taking away Iran's major extortion card against us. On top of that the Iranian regime has slipped so much that there is actually a revolution in progress in country both by anti-Muslim groups and factions inside the Muslim regime. Simply put this was the golden window to nail Iran and through that beat back the terrorist core of the Middle East and put the screws to China and Russia.

This absolutely a benefit to the American people because we have lost our asses on Ukraine and that's thanks in no small part to the logistical supper of Iran to Russia. Finally being able to drain Iran's resources and potentially help overthrow their long term terrorist regime removes a major player from the board that stands against the US and it's allies along with taking a major chess piece from China and Russia so maybe we can finally get the Ukraine war squashed. It doesn't feel like it is an America First decision because it's not immediately obvious and it is a long term investment that will cost heavily in the beginning BUT at the end of the day, it's absolutely a golden opportunity that we cannot afford to throw away if we ever hope to get any amount of long standing peace.

author: @kosh7911
text: I graduated high school in 2000.  I went to Marine Corps Recruit Training on September 11 2000.  One year prior to the terrorist attack.  I joined for love of country and to continue a family tradition.  The terrorist attack exactly one year later did not change anything about how I felt about our country, my Corps or my decision to join.  I did multiple combat deployments across my 20 year career.  I regret none of it.  Were there bad, even terrible, days?  Of course.  However the vast majority of it was a once in a lifetime experience that I wouldn’t replace for anything.  The Marine Corps and the experiences I had have given me nearly everything I have in my life now.  The singular, largest regret I have is that I was not able to physically serve longer.  Getting older is a hell of a thing.
